request.question: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?

title: Sarie Marais
Here We à Go Go Again!
Contemporary folk music
On Top of Old Smoky
The House of the Rising Sun
Song So-hee
Folk Den
Billy Boy
Midnight Special (song)
Min'yō
sentences: "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).

The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.
Here We à Go Go Again!
is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.
The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".
Contemporary folk music refers to a wide variety of genres that emerged in the mid 20th century and afterwards which were associated with traditional folk music.
Starting in the mid-20th century a new form of popular folk music evolved from traditional folk music.
This process and period is called the (second) folk revival and reached a zenith in the 1960s.
The most common name for this new form of music is also "folk music", but is often called "contemporary folk music" or "folk revival music" to make the distinction.
The transition was somewhat centered in the US and is also called the American folk music revival.
Fusion genres such as folk rock, folktronica, and others also evolved within this phenomenon.

While contemporary folk music is a genre generally distinct from traditional folk music, it often shares the same English name, performers and venues as traditional folk music; even individual songs may be a blend of the two.
"On Top of Old Smokey" is a traditional folk song of the United States.
As recorded by The Weavers, the song reached the pop music charts in 1951.
It is catalogued as Roud Folk Song Index No. 414.
In one version the first verse is the following; for more on the words see below.
"The House of the Rising Sun" is a traditional folk song, sometimes called "Rising Sun Blues".
It tells of a life gone wrong in New Orleans; many versions also urge a sibling to avoid the same fate.
The most successful commercial version, recorded in 1964 by British rock group the Animals, was a number one hit on the UK Singles Chart and also in the United States and France.
As a traditional folk song recorded by an electric rock band, it has been described as the "first folk-rock hit".
Song So-hee (born October 20, 1997), is a South Korean singer who specializes in minyo, traditional Korean folk songs.
She has been active since childhood and has often been called a "young female master singer".
She became famous nationwide after she performed "Changbu Taryeong", a well-known Korean traditional folk song, at the KBS National Singing Contest.
She left a deep impression upon the viewers with her performance.
Since then she has been invited to perform at various events, both at home and abroad.
Currently she is a first-year college student at Dankook University (as a traditional music major) and successfully manages her life as a student and a performer.
Folk Den is a folk music website founded in 1995 by Roger McGuinn, former front man of The Byrds.
Hosted at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill's ibiblio, the site is intended to preserve and promote folk music and offers a new folk song on a monthly basis.
Each posting provides an MP3 of a traditional folk song along with a descriptive paragraph, lyrics, guitar chords and related images.
The site has received positive reviews from "The New York Times", the Discovery Channel, and CNET.
"Billy Boy" is a traditional folk song and nursery rhyme found in the United States.
It has a Roud Folk Song Index number of 326.
It is a variant of the traditional English folk song "My Boy Billy", collected by Ralph Vaughan Williams and published by him in 1912 as number 232 in "Novello's School Songs".
The song is very popular with the Orange Order.
"Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.
The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):
Min'yō (民謡 ) is a genre of traditional Japanese music.
The term is a translation of the German word "Volkslied" (folk song) and has only been in use since the twentieth century.

Japanese traditional designations referring to more or less the same genre include "inaka bushi" ("country song") "inaka buri" ("country tune"), "hina uta" ("rural song") and the like, but for most of the people who sang such songs they were simply "uta" (song).

The term min'yō is now sometimes also used to refer to traditional songs of other countries, though a preceding adjective is needed: Furansu min'yō = French folk song; for this reason, many sources in Japanese also feel the need to preface the term with "Nihon": Nihon min'yō = Japanese [traditional] folk song.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?
hop1_docs: Midnight Special (song) | "Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.  The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):

Song Chang-sik | Song Chang-Sik (born 2 February, 1947) is a South Korean folk singer-songwriter.  He is well known for denying the traditional Solfège (both Korean and Western), and re-interpreting the melody on his own.  These melodies can be hardly written with traditional note system, as he "creates" notes that never existed.b

Sarie Marais | "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).  The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.

The Hollow Trees | The Hollow Trees play what they call “folk music for Families.”  It’s high energy acoustic Americana music played with passion, skill, and humor.  They combine the musical styles of bluegrass music, country music, folk music, blues and jazz and put their stamp on many traditional folk songs as well as original compositions.

Folsom Prison Blues | "Folsom Prison Blues" is a song written in 1953 and first recorded in 1955 by American singer-songwriter Johnny Cash.  The song combines elements from two popular folk styles, the train song and the prison song, both of which Cash continued to use for the rest of his career.  It was one of Cash's signature songs.  It was the eleventh track on his debut album "With His Hot and Blue Guitar" and it was also included (same version) on "All Aboard the Blue Train".  A live version, recorded among inmates at Folsom State Prison itself, became a #1 hit on the country music charts in 1968.

Down in the Valley (folk song) | "Down in the Valley", also known as "Birmingham Jail", is a traditional country-blues American folk song.  It has been recorded by many artists, and is included in the "Songs of Expanding America" recordings in the Burl Ives six-album set "Historical America in Song".

You Are My Little Bird | You Are My Little Bird is an album by Elizabeth Mitchell, released in 2006 by Smithsonian Folkways Recordings.  The album is a collection of children's music played in a folk music style.  In addition to versions of folk tunes, it features covers of a variety of songs by other artists, among them "What Goes On" by The Velvet Underground, Neil Young's "Little Wing", and "Three Little Birds" by Bob Marley.  On most of the songs, Mitchell is accompanied by family members.  The liner notes include personal stories from the artist, but no lyrics.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: "Midnight Special" (traditional folk song) and "Maybellene" (song not present in passages).

Facts supported by the passages:
- '"Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song' (passage [1]).
- it is "thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South" (passage [1]).

Ambiguities / missing facts blocking the answer:
- Missing fact: the artist who "created" or is credited with "Maybellene" (no mention of "Maybellene" in the passages).
- Missing fact / ambiguity: whether any single artist "created" or is credited with "Midnight Special" (passage labels it "traditional" and gives no single creator); bridge entity needed: the specific artist who performed/wrote "Maybellene" and whether that same artist is credited with a version of "Midnight Special".

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. "Maybellene (song)" — lede/infobox and "Composition"/"Credits" sections (to get songwriter/artist credited for Maybellene).
2. "Midnight Special (song)" — full article, esp. "Origins"/"History" and "Notable recordings" or "Recorded by" list (to see if the Maybellene artist recorded/adapted it).
3. If Maybellene credited to Chuck Berry, fetch "Chuck Berry" — lede/Discography/"Songs" sections (to check for a "Midnight Special" recording or adaptation).
4. If needed, fetch "Lead Belly" / "Huddie Ledbetter" — lede/"Notable recordings" (candidate associated with "Midnight Special") to compare recordings.
response.hop2_query: artist who created Midnight Special and Maybellene
hop2_docs: Here We à Go Go Again! | Here We à Go Go Again!  is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.  The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".
List of guests appearing on The Midnight Special | This is a partial list of guests who appeared on "The Midnight Special".

The Midnight Special (TV series) | The Midnight Special was an American late-night musical variety series that aired on NBC during the 1970s and early 1980s, created and produced by Burt Sugarman.  It premiered as a special on August 19, 1972, then began its run as a regular series on February 2, 1973; its last episode was on May 1, 1981.  The 90-minute program followed the Friday night edition of "The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson".

Midnighter (2015 comic book) | Midnighter is an American comic book series published by DC Comics and written by Steve Orlando, first issued in June 2015.  It follows the story of a street vigilante, Midnighter, a man with no past who tries to find himself in a new place while he fights for common people against injustices.  The character was originally created by Warren Ellis and artist Bryan Hitch and first appeared as a supporting character in "Stormwatch (Volume 2)" #4 released in February 1998.  The series is also known as Midnighter (Volume 2) to differentiate itself from the character's 20-issue previous series first published by WildStorm in 2006.

Midnight Sun (Marvel Comics) | Midnight Sun (M'Nai) is a fictional character, a former supervillain in the Marvel Comics universe.  He first appeared in "Marvel Special Edition" #16 (February 1974), and was created by Steve Englehart, Jim Starlin, and Al Milgrom.

The Midnight Special (radio) | The Midnight Special is a syndicated radio show broadcast on Chicago, Illinois radio station, WFMT-FM (98.7 MHz) since 1953.  It is a showcase for folk and roots music from historical and contemporary artists.  The show also features comedy sketches and show tunes.  The official description is: "folk music & farce, show tunes & satire, madness & escape."  The show is named after the well known folk song of the same name, which is often associated with Lead Belly, whose version of the song is used as the show's opening theme.  The show title also tied into its time slot, as it was broadcast on WFMT near midnight every Saturday.  It now begins at 9 p.m. and ends at midnight.

Midnight Special (Harry Belafonte album) | Midnight Special is a 1962 album by Jamaican-American singer, Harry Belafonte.  The album notably contains the very first official recording of Bob Dylan, who plays harmonica on the title track.
